quiz.title: Macroeconomics (Quiz 4)
quiz.subtitle: Unit 10 Quiz 4: Phillips Curve Analysis - SRPC, LRPC, and Expectations
quiz.subject: Economics
quiz.topic: Phillips Curve, SRPC Shifts, LRPC, NAIRU, Expectations, Supply Shocks, Stagflation

questions.question: If the unemployment rate rises and the inflation rate falls, while the natural unemployment rate and the expected inflation rate remain constant, along which of the following curves is the economy moving?
options: aggregate demand curve
long-run aggregate supply curve
Friedman curve
short-run Phillips curve
Phelps-Friedman curve
questions.explanation: When the natural rate and expected inflation are constant, the SRPC is stationary. A rise in unemployment and fall in inflation represents a movement down along the existing short-run Phillips curve, typically caused by a decrease in aggregate demand.

questions.question: How does an increase in the expected rate of inflation influence the Phillips curves?
options: The short-run Phillips curve shifts downward.
The short-run Phillips curve shifts upward.
The long-run Phillips curve shifts rightward.
The long-run Phillips curve shifts leftward.
The long-run Phillips curve shifts upward.
questions.explanation: An increase in expected inflation shifts the SRPC upward (or rightward) because workers and firms build higher inflation expectations into wage and price contracts. The LRPC is unaffected because it depends only on the natural rate of unemployment.

questions.question: Given the graph shown, if the current inflation rate is 4 percent a year, what is the current unemployment rate?
options: 4 percent
6 percent
3 percent
2 percent
7 percent
questions.explanation: On the SRPC, locate the point where the inflation rate equals 4%. Reading down to the horizontal axis gives the corresponding unemployment rate, which is 4%.

questions.question: Given the graph shown, what happens when the expected inflation rate changes to 3 percent a year?
options: The short-run Phillips curve shifts upward and the long-run Phillips curve does not change.
The short-run Phillips curve shifts downward and the long-run Phillips curve does not change.
The short-run Phillips curve shifts upward and the long-run Phillips curve shifts leftward.
The short-run Phillips curve shifts upward and the long-run Phillips curve shifts rightward.
The short-run Phillips curve shifts downward and the long-run Phillips curve shifts rightward.
questions.explanation: If the new expected inflation rate (3%) is higher than the previous expected rate implied by the graph, the SRPC shifts upward. The LRPC remains unchanged because the natural rate of unemployment has not changed.

questions.question: Along a specific SRPC, which of the following variables is assumed to be held constant?
options: The actual inflation rate
The unemployment rate
The expected inflation rate
Aggregate demand
The natural rate of unemployment
questions.explanation: A specific short-run Phillips curve is drawn for a given expected inflation rate. Changes in expected inflation shift the entire SRPC. Movement along a given SRPC occurs when actual inflation changes due to demand shocks, while expected inflation remains constant.

questions.question: The point at which the SRPC intersects the LRPC identifies which specific economic state?
options: A recessionary gap
An inflationary gap
Potential GDP / Full employment
Stagflation
Deflationary equilibrium
questions.explanation: The LRPC is vertical at the natural rate of unemployment. The SRPC crosses the LRPC at the point where actual inflation equals expected inflation. At this intersection, the unemployment rate equals the natural rate, meaning the economy is at potential GDP (full employment).

questions.question: Why is the LRPC represented as a vertical line at the natural rate of unemployment?
options: Because inflation expectations adjust instantly to policy changes.
Because in the long run, there is no trade-off between inflation and unemployment.
Because wages are perfectly sticky in the long run.
Because the central bank targets a specific unemployment rate.
Because aggregate supply is horizontal in the long run.
questions.explanation: In the long run, nominal wages and prices fully adjust to any change in aggregate demand or inflation. The unemployment rate returns to the natural rate determined by structural and frictional factors, regardless of the inflation rate. Thus, no long-run trade-off exists.

questions.question: If the natural rate of unemployment increases, how do the SRPC and LRPC respond?
options: Only the LRPC shifts rightward.
Only the SRPC shifts rightward.
Both the LRPC and SRPC shift rightward.
Both the LRPC and SRPC shift leftward.
Neither curve shifts; movement occurs along the SRPC.
questions.explanation: The LRPC is anchored at the natural rate, so it shifts rightward. Since the SRPC is centered around the LRPC, the entire SRPC also shifts rightward to reflect the higher natural rate of unemployment.

questions.question: A movement up along a stationary SRPC is most likely caused by an increase in which variable?
options: Expected inflation
The natural rate of unemployment
Aggregate demand
Production costs (supply shock)
Labor productivity
questions.explanation: Movement along a stationary SRPC occurs when aggregate demand changes. An increase in AD raises actual inflation and lowers unemployment, moving the economy up and left along the existing SRPC.

questions.question: An adverse supply shock (e.g., a sudden spike in oil prices) would cause what change to the Phillips curve diagram?
options: Movement down along the SRPC
Movement up along the SRPC
The SRPC shifts upward (rightward)
The SRPC shifts downward (leftward)
The LRPC shifts rightward
questions.explanation: An adverse supply shock raises production costs, increasing inflation at every level of unemployment. This shifts the short-run Phillips curve upward (or rightward). Stagflation results: higher inflation and higher unemployment simultaneously.

questions.question: If the government implements an expansionary monetary policy that is fully anticipated by the public, how does the economy move in the Phillips curve model?
options: Movement up and left along the existing SRPC.
The SRPC shifts downward and unemployment falls.
The economy jumps directly to a higher inflation rate with no change in unemployment.
The economy moves down and right along the existing SRPC.
The LRPC shifts leftward.
questions.explanation: If the policy is fully anticipated, workers and firms immediately adjust their inflation expectations and nominal wages. The SRPC shifts upward instantly. The economy moves from the original equilibrium to a new one on the LRPC with higher inflation but the same natural rate of unemployment.

questions.question: What would cause a downward shift of the SRPC without affecting the LRPC? SELECT ALL THAT APPLY.
options: A decrease in the natural rate of unemployment
A decrease in expected inflation
A favorable supply shock (e.g., falling oil prices)
A reduction in Government spending
An increase in aggregate demand
questions.explanation: Both a decrease in expected inflation and a favorable supply shock reduce inflation at every unemployment rate, shifting the SRPC downward (leftward). The LRPC remains unchanged because the natural rate of unemployment has not changed. A decrease in the natural rate shifts both the LRPC and SRPC leftward. A reduction in government spending and an increase in aggregate demand both represent changes in aggregate demand, which cause movement along the existing SRPC rather than shifting the curve itself.

questions.question: When the actual unemployment rate is equal to the natural unemployment rate, what can be concluded about the inflation rate?
options: Inflation must be zero.
Inflation must be rising.
Inflation equals expected inflation.
Inflation must be falling.
Inflation is at its maximum possible level.
questions.explanation: According to the expectations-augmented Phillips curve: $\pi = \pi^e - \beta(u - u_n)$. When $u = u_n$, actual inflation ($\pi$) equals expected inflation ($\pi^e$). The inflation rate itself could be high, low, or stable, but it matches expectations.

questions.question: If the actual unemployment rate is lower than the natural rate, what is the expected long-term pressure on the SRPC?
options: The SRPC will shift downward as workers accept lower wages.
The SRPC will shift upward as inflation expectations rise.
The SRPC will remain stationary as the economy self-corrects without inflation.
The LRPC will shift leftward to match the lower unemployment.
The economy will move permanently down the SRPC.
questions.explanation: When $u < u_n$, actual inflation exceeds expected inflation. Over time, workers and firms adjust their expectations upward. The SRPC shifts upward until unemployment returns to the natural rate.

questions.question: Define the NAIRU (Non-Accelerating Inflation Rate of Unemployment) in the context of the LRPC.
options: The unemployment rate at which inflation is zero.
The unemployment rate at which actual inflation equals expected inflation.
The lowest possible unemployment rate achievable through expansionary policy.
The unemployment rate that maximizes potential GDP.
The unemployment rate that exists during a severe recession.
questions.explanation: NAIRU is synonymous with the natural rate of unemployment. It is the unemployment rate at which inflation is stable (neither accelerating nor decelerating) because actual inflation equals expected inflation. The LRPC is vertical at the NAIRU.

questions.question: Suppose the economy is at the natural rate. If the central bank successfully lowers inflation expectations, what happens?
options: The SRPC shifts downward, lowering actual inflation without changing unemployment.
The economy moves down along the existing SRPC to lower unemployment.
The LRPC shifts leftward, reducing the natural rate.
The SRPC shifts upward, causing stagflation.
The economy moves up along the LRPC to higher inflation.
questions.explanation: Lower inflation expectations shift the SRPC downward. Since the economy starts at the natural rate, the new equilibrium has the same unemployment rate but a lower actual inflation rate. This is the ideal 'soft landing' scenario.

questions.question: According to the SRPC, what is the short-run trade-off faced by policymakers attempting to reduce unemployment?
options: Lower unemployment requires accepting higher inflation.
Lower unemployment requires reducing the money supply.
Lower unemployment can only be achieved by raising taxes.
Lower unemployment requires reducing government spending.
There is no trade-off; unemployment can be reduced without inflation.
questions.explanation: The SRPC is downward sloping, illustrating the short-run trade-off: expansionary policy can lower unemployment below the natural rate, but only at the cost of higher inflation.

questions.question: Why does the trade-off between inflation and unemployment disappear on the LRPC?
options: Because the central bank loses control of the money supply in the long run.
Because wages and prices are perfectly flexible in the long run, and expectations fully adjust.
Because fiscal policy becomes ineffective in the long run.
Because unemployment always returns to zero.
Because inflation expectations become fixed by law.
questions.explanation: In the long run, nominal wages and prices fully adjust to any inflation rate. Workers' expectations catch up to reality, so real wages return to their equilibrium level. The unemployment rate returns to the natural rate determined by real factors, regardless of the inflation rate.

questions.question: If a country experiences stagflation, how is this illustrated using the Phillips curve framework?
options: A movement down along the existing SRPC.
A movement up along the existing SRPC.
A rightward (upward) shift of the SRPC.
A leftward (downward) shift of the SRPC.
A rightward shift of the LRPC.
questions.explanation: Stagflation—rising inflation and rising unemployment—cannot be explained by movement along a stationary SRPC. It requires an adverse supply shock that shifts the SRPC upward (rightward), moving the economy to a point with both higher inflation and higher unemployment.

questions.question: What is the impact of an increase in unemployment benefits on the position of the LRPC?
options: The LRPC shifts leftward.
The LRPC shifts rightward.
The LRPC shifts upward.
The LRPC remains unchanged; only the SRPC shifts.
The LRPC becomes downward sloping.
questions.explanation: More generous unemployment benefits reduce the incentive to search for work, increasing frictional unemployment. This raises the natural rate of unemployment, shifting the vertical LRPC rightward.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered the Phillips curve framework, the distinction between SRPC and LRPC, and the role of expectations.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! You understand the core mechanics. Remember: Expected inflation shifts SRPC. The natural rate shifts both SRPC and LRPC.
scoreMessages.50: A fair attempt. Focus on: 1) SRPC slopes down (short-run trade-off). 2) LRPC is vertical at the natural rate. 3) Expectations shift SRPC. 4) Supply shocks shift SRPC.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Remember: Movement along SRPC = demand shocks. Shift of SRPC = expectations or supply shocks. Shift of LRPC = change in natural rate.
